Party_Details.Name: Griffin Travel Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Griffin Travel Pte Ltd is a travel agency providing travel services to the marine, offshore and cruise industries as at 16 October 2014.
Griffin Travel Pte Ltd has two shareholders, Griffin Marine Travel (Cyprus) Limited and Griffin Global Group Limited.
Griffin Global Group Limited is wholly owned by Griffin Bidco Limited.
Griffin Bidco Limited is wholly owned by Griffin Global Holdco Limited.
The Griffin group of companies consists of numerous entities around the world, including entities referred to by geographical location such as Griffin India.
On 1 June 2002, Griffin Travel Pte Ltd issued a Staff Manual containing regulations and individual policies, later amended on 1 July 2002.

Section 1.1 of the Staff Manual states that the rules and regulations in the manual constitute part of the employment contract for staff of Global Marine Travel Services Pte Ltd, the former name of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.

Section 2.3 of the Staff Manual sets out a policy that an employee upon signing the contract of employment shall commit full-time service to the company and requires written permission before taking up part-time or secondary employment or engagements with other companies.
Section 2.5(1) of the Staff Manual states that no employee may conduct commercial activity or business or use company facilities for private purposes on company premises.

Section 2.5(2) of the Staff Manual states that employees are not permitted to hold directorships or formal positions in other companies without prior knowledge and written approval from the directors, and requires disclosure of certain directorships or shareholdings held by spouses or dependants that may result in conflicts of interest.

Section 2.5(3) of the Staff Manual contains confidentiality provisions restricting employees from divulging company business, finance, transactions, affairs or dealings to third parties during or after employment without written permission of management.

On 1 January 2009, Griffin Travel Pte Ltd entered into written employment contracts with all defendants except Joanna Kaunang, and these contracts contained an entire agreement clause and a clause 15 referring to a Policy Manual.

Clause 1 of the 1 January 2009 employment contracts states that the agreement is effective as of 01-Jan-09 and replaces all other employment agreements, whether oral or written, and that previous agreements, correspondence, negotiations, representations, explanations, statements, promises or guarantees are excluded.

Clause 15 of the 1 January 2009 employment contracts states that the employment agreement shall be governed by the Policy Manual of the company, which may be amended from time to time and forms part of the contract of employment, and that employees may see these regulations on application to their immediate manager.
Employment agreements dated 1 January 2006 for Leny Widjaja, Pereira Rahul Anthony, Ng Choo Geok Adella and Gwee Bee Ting Annie expressly referred to a Staff Manual.
With effect from 1 October 2011, Ali Hussain held the position of managing director of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
In 2008, Nagender Rao Chilkuri held a 20% shareholding in Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.

As part of a corporate restructuring exercise in 2008 to facilitate a partial or full sale of the Griffin group, Nagender Rao Chilkuri transferred his shares in Griffin Travel Pte Ltd to Griffin Global Group Limited in return for shares in Griffin Global Group Limited.
In 2009, the Griffin group entered into talks with Inflexion Private Equity Partners LLP regarding investment in the group.

On 16 December 2009, under a subscription agreement between Griffin Global Holdco Limited, Griffin Bidco Limited and Nagender Rao Chilkuri, Nagender, through Signature Sparks Pte Ltd, subscribed to a 9.1% stake in Griffin Global Holdco Limited and a GBP 1.6 million loan note issued by Griffin Bidco Limited.
On 16 December 2009, Inflexion Private Equity Partners LLP purchased 25% of the shares in Griffin Global Holdco Limited.

Sometime in late 2009, the management of the Griffin group designated Nagender Rao Chilkuri as the next Global chief executive officer of the Griffin group and publicised this within the group and to third parties.
By late 2009, Nagender Rao Chilkuri held the position of managing director of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd and was also a director of Griffin Global Holdco Limited and Griffin Bidco Limited.

As at early 2011, George Boyes held the position of chief executive officer of Griffin Global Group Limited and was a director of Griffin Global Holdco Limited and Griffin Bidco Limited and a shareholder of Griffin Global Holdco Limited.
As at early 2011, Agyapal Khuman was managing director of Griffin India and a director of Griffin Global Holdco Limited and Griffin Bidco Limited.

Until his resignation on 27 August 2011, Marcus Hebblethwaite was chief financial officer of the Griffin group, a director of Griffin Global Holdco Limited and Griffin Bidco Limited, and a shareholder of Griffin Global Holdco Limited.
As at early 2011, Alister Beveridge was chief information officer of Griffin Global Group Limited.
As at early 2011, Piyush Shukla was financial controller of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
As at early 2011, Andy Chua was a director of D’Perception Singapore Pte Ltd, a renovation contractor that had previously renovated premises for Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
As at early 2011, Toh Ching Wah was a director at KPMG Forensic, a division of KPMG Services Pte Ltd.
As at early 2011, Rita Ganes was a client services manager of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
As at early 2011, Indraneel Fuke was a director of BHEA Knowledge Technologies (P) Ltd, an IT firm based in India.
As at early 2011, Ajay Satam Jagannath was an employee of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
As at early 2011, Sandeep Singhania was a director of Lantone Systems Pte Ltd, an IT service provider to Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
As at early 2011, Ciocan Cornel was a director and shareholder of Net Vision SEA Pte Ltd, which had previously provided software services to Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
As at early 2011, Tay Tat Hwa was an associate director at KordaMentha Forensic Pte Ltd.

On 23 September 2010, after a board meeting, Nagender Rao Chilkuri sent an email to George Boyes, Agyapal Khuman, Marcus Hebblethwaite and others stating that recent events had made his continued involvement with the organisation untenable and that it was timely for him to move on.

On 23 September 2010, Nagender Rao Chilkuri sent a follow-up email stating that he agreed it would be appropriate to diffuse the situation and to meet after a couple of days to discuss and bring the matter to a conclusion.

On 7 October 2010, after a meeting in Mumbai, George Boyes emailed Christian Hamilton and Gareth Healy of Inflexion stating that he had a lengthy discussion with Nagender Rao Chilkuri and that things were returning to normality, and that he still had to discuss Nagender's position going forward, including a suggestion for Nagender to start as a regional head for Asia with added responsibility for global operations.

On 30 October 2010, Christian Hamilton of Inflexion emailed Gareth Healy and major shareholders of the Griffin group, including Nagender Rao Chilkuri, attaching a presentation that stated recruitment for a new Global CEO would start immediately and was take approximately six to nine months.

On 18 November 2010, during a telephone conversation with Christian Hamilton, Nagender Rao Chilkuri stated that he was very bitter about the Global CEO succession issue and that if the transition from the current CEO to a new CEO did not happen, he was ready to leave.

On 23 November 2010, Dick Porter emailed global shareholders ahead of a 25 November 2010 board meeting stating that planning for CEO recruitment would begin immediately and that the process would be open to internal and external candidates.

On 26 November 2010, shareholder Bob Westerndarp emailed global shareholders stating that the search for a new Global CEO needed to start immediately and that Nagender Rao Chilkuri had expressed that he had lost interest in the business.
On 26 November 2010, Nagender Rao Chilkuri emailed in response that he would earn his pay until the day he left and asked to be advised on the process for disengaging from Griffin.

On 12 January 2011, at a shareholders' meeting in Athens, the management shareholders of the Griffin group agreed not to start recruitment for a new CFO and CEO until discussions on a potential merger with VIA were concluded, and appointed Nagender Rao Chilkuri as chairman of the management committee of the Griffin group.

On 14 January 2011, George Boyes emailed Dick Porter stating that the partners had decided not to proceed with hiring a CEO or CFO at that stage and that they would reconsider if the VIA merger was abandoned, and that Nagender Rao Chilkuri had decided to stay.

In February 2011, George Boyes and Nagender Rao Chilkuri travelled together to Houston in the United States and to Brazil to assess operations of Griffin entities there and to consider possible improvements.

On 24 March 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri emailed shareholders of the Griffin group stating that the way the succession had been offered and then changed placed him in a difficult situation and that any changes in the CEO's office would affect his decisions going forward.

On 28 March 2011, George Boyes emailed Nagender Rao Chilkuri stating that he had previously presented Nagender as the person to take over as CEO to internal managers and external parties and that it had been a painful decision to change that plan, and that in his view Nagender was not ready to become the global CEO of Griffin at that time.

Sometime in May 2011, during a phone conversation, Dick Porter and Agyapal Khuman informed Nagender Rao Chilkuri that George Boyes wished to step down later in 2011 and that a search was beginning for an external candidate to replace him as Global CEO.
On 9 June 2011, George Boyes forwarded to global shareholders an email he had sent to Gareth Healy on 2 June 2011 which included a statement that activity for recruiting a CEO had to be postponed.
On 21 June 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri resigned from the board of Griffin India.
On 22 June 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri resigned from the Griffin Global board.
On 16 August 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri tendered his resignation as managing director of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, with an agreed last day of employment of 15 January 2012.
On 19 September 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri resigned from the board of directors of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
In January 2011, Leny Widjaja created an online discussion group at new-venture-2011@googlegroups.com to facilitate communications about incorporating a company later named Quest Horizon Pte Ltd.
On 6 January 2011, Joanna Kaunang emailed the Google Group listing several proposed company names and asking for input, including the suggestion 'Quest Horizon'.
On 6 January 2011, in reply to Joanna Kaunang, Nagender Rao Chilkuri wrote that 'Quest Horizon looks good'.

On 21 February 2011, Quest Horizon Pte Ltd was incorporated with an initial paid-up capital of SGD 250,000, with directors Gwee Bee Ting Annie and Ng Choo Geok Adella and shareholders Joanna Kaunang (10%), Gwee Bee Ting Annie (10%), Ng Choo Geok Adella (35%) and Madhu Kumar (45%).
In March 2011, Quest Horizon Pte Ltd engaged IP Consultants Pte Ltd as its corporate secretary and Prasad liaised with IP Consultants on incorporation-related matters.
On 25 March 2011, an email account under the Quest Horizon domain was created for Nagender Rao Chilkuri, and access records show it was last accessed on 31 March 2011.
In late 2010, Ciocan Cornel discussed with Nagender Rao Chilkuri a proposal to collaborate on setting up a business-to-business booking system relating to travel procurement.

In early March 2011, Ciocan Cornel held a main discussion with Nagender Rao Chilkuri about the proposed travel booking system project and also approached Sandeep Singhania, who agreed to participate with a small stake.

Around mid-March 2011, after discussions, Nagender Rao Chilkuri declined Ciocan Cornel’s proposal to participate in the travel booking system project and suggested that Cornel approach Joanna Kaunang, Gwee Bee Ting Annie and Ng Choo Geok Adella.

By late March 2011, Ciocan Cornel obtained agreement from Joanna Kaunang, Gwee Bee Ting Annie and Ng Choo Geok Adella to participate in the new software venture later incorporated as Niado Technology Pte Ltd.
Around mid-April 2011, Ng Choo Geok Adella and Gwee Bee Ting Annie began sourcing for business premises for Niado Technology Pte Ltd and Quest Horizon Pte Ltd.
By 25 April 2011, a decision was made by the participants to take two adjacent office units at Changi Business Park for use by Quest Horizon Pte Ltd and Niado Technology Pte Ltd.

On 27 April 2011, Niado Technology Pte Ltd was incorporated with a paid-up capital of SGD 80,000, with directors including Gwee Bee Ting Annie, Ciocan Cornel and Sandeep Singhania, and shareholdings of 45% for Quest Horizon Pte Ltd, 45% for Ciocan Cornel and 10% for Sandeep Singhania.
By May 2011, the shareholders of Quest Horizon Pte Ltd had increased its share capital from SGD 250,000 to SGD 375,000 to fund rental and renovation costs for office premises.

On 31 May 2011, Quest Horizon Pte Ltd signed a tenancy agreement for unit 08-03 at Changi Business Park and Niado Technology Pte Ltd signed a tenancy agreement for unit 08-04 at the same location, with a combined monthly rent of SGD 11,323.11.

From June 2011, Quest Horizon Pte Ltd’s unit at Changi Business Park was shared by Net Vision SEA Pte Ltd, BHEA Technologies, EGSoft Pte Ltd and Signature Sparks Pte Ltd, with these entities contributing to rent without a premium.
In or around May 2011, Leny Widjaja, who was serving notice after resigning from Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, actively assisted Quest Horizon Pte Ltd with renovation of the Changi Business Park offices.

Around May 2011, Leny Widjaja contacted Nagender Rao Chilkuri to obtain contact details for D’Perception Singapore Pte Ltd to assist with renovation of the Changi Business Park offices for Quest Horizon Pte Ltd and Niado Technology Pte Ltd.
Around May 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri telephoned Andy Chua of D’Perception Singapore Pte Ltd to discuss renovation work for the Changi Business Park offices, referring to the work as the QH Project.
In mid-2011, Quest Horizon Pte Ltd incurred renovation costs for the Changi Business Park offices in the approximate range of SGD 130,000 to SGD 140,000.
In mid-2011, Quest Horizon Pte Ltd purchased 26 Dell computer monitors intended for use in the Changi Business Park offices.
As at 2011, Signature Sparks Pte Ltd was an investment holding company through which Nagender Rao Chilkuri held shares in Griffin Global Holdco Limited and a loan note in Griffin Bidco Limited.
As at 2011, Narra Gouri Prasad was the brother-in-law of Nagender Rao Chilkuri.
As at early 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri was managing director of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd and a shareholder in Griffin Global Holdco Limited through Signature Sparks Pte Ltd.
On 2 February 2011, Leny Widjaja resigned from her employment with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, with her last day of employment and resignation from the board of directors on 30 June 2011.
On 1 March 2011, Narra Gouri Prasad resigned from his position as Vice President – Finance at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, with his last day of employment on 31 August 2011.
On 24 August 2011, Ng Choo Geok Adella resigned from her position as director of operations at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
On 12 September 2011, Gwee Bee Ting Annie resigned from her position as general manager of operations at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
On 21 September 2011, Joanna Kaunang resigned from her employment with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
On 22 September 2011, Pereira Rahul Anthony resigned from his position as manager of operations at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the conduct of former senior employees and directors in incorporating and participating in new entities, arranging office premises and investments, and resigning from Griffin Travel Pte Ltd before and during 2011 is associated with competitive activity or conflicts with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd’s interests, and whether Griffin Travel Pte Ltd’s subsequent termination of their employment and related classifications under group share and loan arrangements are justified.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Singapore employment and company law, employees owe an implied duty of fidelity, including duties to act honestly and faithfully and not to engage in actual competitive activity with their employer during employment, while directors owe obligations to act in good faith in what they consider to be the best interests of the company, to avoid unauthorised conflicts between personal interests and company interests, and to refrain from exploiting corporate opportunities for personal benefit without informed consent. Preparatory steps towards future competition may be permissible for employees if they do not amount to actual competition or misuse of confidential information, whereas directors are stricter standards regarding conflicts and disclosure of matters that materially affect the company.
Party_Details.Application: The timeline shows that Quest Horizon Pte Ltd was incorporated on 21 February 2011 with share capital funded by four individuals, that Niado Technology Pte Ltd was incorporated on 27 April 2011 with Quest Horizon as a 45% shareholder, and that office premises at Changi Business Park were leased from 31 May 2011 for use by Quest Horizon, Niado and other entities. These steps occurred while several individuals, including Nagender Rao Chilkuri, Joanna Kaunang, Gwee Bee Ting Annie, Ng Choo Geok Adella and Narra Gouri Prasad, still held roles at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd. The communications and board events from late 2010 to mid-2011 indicate ongoing discussions about succession to the Global CEO role, Nagender’s changing intentions regarding remaining with the group, and his eventual resignations from various boards and from Griffin Travel Pte Ltd. The same period includes the creation of the Google Group, selection of the Quest Horizon name, engagement of D’Perception for renovation, and sharing of office space and costs among Quest Horizon, Niado and related companies. These factual linkages frame the question of whether the incorporation and funding of the new entities, the use of shared premises and resources, and the timing of resignations are consistent with permissible preparatory activity or constitute competitive conduct or conflicted exploitation of opportunities in light of the duties owed by employees and directors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment analyses whether the Staff Manual was incorporated into the 1 January 2009 employment contracts, concluding that the reference to a Policy Manual and the entire agreement clause meant the Staff Manual was not incorporated. It then considers which defendants owed fiduciary duties, finding that only directors such as Nagender and Leny did, and that Adella and Prasad, although senior, were part of middle management and did not owe fiduciary obligations. The court adopts the approach that directors do not have a free-standing duty of disclosure but that non-disclosure can amount to breach of fiduciary duty where motivated by bad faith. For employees, the court follows authorities such as Smile Inc and Ranson, holding that the implied duty is one of mutual trust and confidence and fidelity, not a broad duty of good faith, and that employees may take preparatory steps to compete after employment, provided they do not engage in actual competition or misuse confidential information. The court rejects a general implied duty for non-fiduciary employees to report their own or colleagues’ wrongdoing absent express contractual terms. On the alleged masterplan, the court examines in detail the Global CEO succession events, including emails and meetings from September 2010 through mid-2011, and concludes that Nagender likely only formed a firm decision to resign between May and July 2011, not as early as February 2011 when Quest Horizon was incorporated. This undermines the allegation that he masterminded a revenge plan from that earlier date. The court then evaluates the formation and activities of Quest Horizon and Niado, including the pooling of funds, the initial idea of property investment, the approach by Cornel regarding a software venture, the incorporation details, the leasing and renovation of the Changi Business Park offices, and the sharing of space and costs with other entities. It finds that many of the plaintiff’s suspicious circumstances can be explained consistently with Quest Horizon being an investment vehicle and Niado being a start-up software venture, and that the evidence does not support the assertion that Madhu was a proxy for Nagender or that Quest Horizon was clearly the holding company of a conglomerate controlled by Nagender. The court notes Andy Chua’s impression that the renovation project was a private project by Nagender and Leny and that Nagender wanted it kept from Griffin, but treats this as one factor among many rather than conclusive proof of a concealed masterplan. Ultimately, the judgment addresses liability issues for alleged breaches of duties and wrongful dismissal, but the excerpt provided stops before the final holdings on each claim and counterclaim, so the specific outcomes on liability and the counterclaims, including the Bad Leaver classification and wrongful dismissal allegations, are not fully set out in the text supplied.

Party_Details.Name: Nagender Rao Chilkuri

Facts.Text: In 2008, Nagender Rao Chilkuri held a 20% shareholding in Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
By late 2009, Nagender Rao Chilkuri was managing director of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd and a director of Griffin Global Holdco Limited and Griffin Bidco Limited.

In 2008, as part of a corporate restructuring of the Griffin group, Nagender Rao Chilkuri transferred his shares in Griffin Travel Pte Ltd to Griffin Global Group Limited in exchange for shares in Griffin Global Group Limited.

On 16 December 2009, through his investment holding company Signature Sparks Pte Ltd, Nagender Rao Chilkuri subscribed to a 9.1% shareholding in Griffin Global Holdco Limited and a GBP 1.6 million loan note issued by Griffin Bidco Limited.
Sometime in late 2009, the Griffin group designated Nagender Rao Chilkuri as the next Global chief executive officer and publicised this designation internally and to external parties.

On 23 September 2010, after a heated exchange with CEO George Boyes at a Griffin group board meeting, Nagender Rao Chilkuri emailed senior colleagues stating that recent events made his continued involvement untenable and that it was timely for him to move on.
On 23 September 2010, Nagender Rao Chilkuri sent a follow-up email stating that he agreed to diffuse the situation and to meet after a couple of days to discuss and bring the matter to a conclusion.

On 7 October 2010, following a meeting in Mumbai, CEO George Boyes emailed Inflexion representatives stating that he had a lengthy discussion with Nagender Rao Chilkuri and that he still had to discuss Nagender's position going forward, including a suggestion that Nagender start as regional head for Asia with added global operational responsibilities.
On 12 January 2011, at a shareholders' meeting in Athens, Nagender Rao Chilkuri was appointed chairman of the management committee of the Griffin group.
In February 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri travelled with CEO George Boyes to Houston and Brazil to review operations of Griffin entities and consider improvements.

On 24 March 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri emailed Griffin group shareholders stating that the way the succession had been offered and then changed placed him in a negative personal and professional situation and that changes in the CEO's office would affect his decisions going forward.

On 28 March 2011, CEO George Boyes emailed Nagender Rao Chilkuri acknowledging that he had previously presented Nagender as his successor to internal and external parties and explaining his reasons for deciding that Nagender was not ready to become global CEO at that time.

In May 2011, during a phone call, Dick Porter and Agyapal Khuman informed Nagender Rao Chilkuri that George Boyes wished to step down later in 2011 and that a search was beginning for an external candidate to replace him as Global CEO.
On 21 June 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri resigned from the board of Griffin India.
On 22 June 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri resigned from the Griffin Global board.

On 22 July 2011, Marcus Hebblethwaite telephoned Nagender Rao Chilkuri to inform him of the possible appointment of Simon Morse as executive chairman of the Griffin group board, and Nagender recorded this call.

After 22 July 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri requested Lantone Systems Pte Ltd to retrieve the recording of his call with Marcus Hebblethwaite and other phone recordings from Griffin Travel Pte Ltd’s phone extensions.
On 16 August 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri resigned as managing director of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, with an agreed last day of employment of 15 January 2012.
On 19 September 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri resigned from the board of directors of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
On 21 November 2011, Griffin Travel Pte Ltd sent a letter to Nagender Rao Chilkuri terminating his employment on allegations of gross misconduct and dishonesty.
As at 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri was related by marriage to Narra Gouri Prasad, who is his brother-in-law.

In early March 2011, Ciocan Cornel approached Nagender Rao Chilkuri with a proposal to collaborate on a business-to-business travel booking system project, and Nagender declined participation about one to two weeks later and suggested that Cornel approach Joanna Kaunang, Gwee Bee Ting Annie and Ng Choo Geok Adella.

On 6 January 2011, in an email exchange on the new-venture-2011 Google Group, Nagender Rao Chilkuri responded to a list of proposed company names from Joanna Kaunang by stating that 'Quest Horizon looks good'.
On 25 March 2011, a Quest Horizon Pte Ltd email account was created for Nagender Rao Chilkuri, which records show was last accessed on 31 March 2011.

Around May 2011, Nagender Rao Chilkuri telephoned Andy Chua of D’Perception Singapore Pte Ltd to discuss renovation work for the Changi Business Park offices associated with Quest Horizon Pte Ltd, referring to the work as the QH Project and indicating that it was a private project.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, during his tenure as managing director and director within the Griffin group, and while holding share and loan interests through Signature Sparks Pte Ltd, Nagender Rao Chilkuri’s involvement in discussions about new ventures, communications about succession, participation in or assistance to entities such as Quest Horizon Pte Ltd and Niado Technology Pte Ltd, and his resignations and subsequent termination, are consistent with his contractual and director-level obligations or give rise to liability and to or against him in respect of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd’s claims and his counterclaims, including his classification as a Bad Leaver and dismissal.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors of a company are generally act in good faith in what they consider to be the best interests of the company, to avoid unauthorised conflicts between their personal interests and the company’s interests, and to refrain from exploiting corporate opportunities for themselves without informed consent. Senior employees owe an implied duty of fidelity and mutual trust and confidence, which includes acting honestly and not engaging in actual competition with their employer during employment or misusing confidential information, while allowing some preparatory steps for future competition. Contractual terms, including entire agreement clauses and references to policy manuals, determine the scope of express obligations, and share and loan arrangements may include leaver provisions that depend on the circumstances of departure and any established misconduct.
Party_Details.Application: Nagender Rao Chilkuri’s role as managing director and director within the Griffin group, his shareholding and loan note interests through Signature Sparks Pte Ltd, and his designation as the next Global CEO placed him in a senior position with significant responsibilities. From late 2010 through mid-2011, he exchanged emails and held discussions with other senior figures about succession, expressed dissatisfaction with changes to succession plans, and at times indicated a desire to disengage, while also accepting appointment as chairman of the management committee and undertaking global review trips. During the same period, he participated in the new-venture-2011 Google Group, commented on the proposed name Quest Horizon, declined direct participation in Cornel’s software venture and redirected Cornel to other colleagues, and had a Quest Horizon email account created. He also contacted D’Perception’s director to arrange renovation for the Changi Business Park offices associated with Quest Horizon and described the project as private. He resigned from the boards of Griffin India and Griffin Global in June 2011, resigned as managing director of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd in August 2011, and was later terminated by Griffin Travel Pte Ltd in November 2011. These facts provide the basis for assessing whether his conduct remained within permissible preparatory activity and assistance to colleagues or whether it intersected with corporate opportunities or conflicts in a way that engages director-level and employment obligations, and for evaluating the contractual consequences of his departure under the group’s share and loan arrangements.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treats Nagender Rao Chilkuri as a key figure and examines whether he orchestrated a masterplan to harm Griffin Travel Pte Ltd after being passed over for the Global CEO role. It reconstructs the chronology of succession-related events, including his initial designation as successor, subsequent reconsideration by management and Inflexion, his confrontations with CEO George Boyes, and multiple emails and meetings from September 2010 to mid-2011. The judge concludes that while Nagender was upset and at times threatened to leave, he did not form a firm decision to resign as early as February 2011, when Quest Horizon was incorporated, and likely only made an irrevocable decision to quit between May and July 2011, with resignations from the Griffin India and Griffin Global boards in June 2011 as key indicators. This undermines the allegation that he masterminded a revenge-driven conglomerate from February 2011. The court also analyses his interactions with Cornel’s software proposal, noting that Nagender declined to participate and suggested Cornel approach other staff, and finds it inconsistent with the theory that he used Quest Horizon as a holding company for a conglomerate he controlled. The judge considers evidence from D’Perception’s director that Nagender referred to the renovation as a private QH Project and wanted it kept from Griffin, treating this as a factor that may suggest closer involvement but not conclusive proof of a concealed masterplan. The judgment further addresses Nagender’s fiduciary duties as a director, the implied duties of fidelity as an employee, and the legal framework for assessing whether his preparatory steps and assistance to colleagues crossed into impermissible competition or conflicted exploitation of opportunities. It also notes his share and loan interests via Signature Sparks Pte Ltd and the Bad Leaver classification, but the excerpt does not set out the final determinations on his liability, wrongful dismissal claim, or leaver status.

Party_Details.Name: Joanna Kaunang

Facts.Text: Until mid-2011, Joanna Kaunang was customer service manager at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd’s Perth office.
Sometime in July 2011, Joanna Kaunang returned from Perth to Singapore to work at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.

After returning to Singapore in July 2011, Joanna Kaunang assumed the position of acting general manager of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd and was positioned to become managing director upon Nagender Rao Chilkuri’s departure.
On 21 September 2011, Joanna Kaunang resigned from her employment with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.

In January 2011, Joanna Kaunang asked Leny Widjaja to set up the new-venture-2011@googlegroups.com Google Group to facilitate communications about incorporating Quest Horizon Pte Ltd while Joanna was based in Sydney.
On 6 January 2011, Joanna Kaunang emailed the Google Group listing potential company names, including 'Quest Horizon', and asked for input.
On 21 February 2011, Quest Horizon Pte Ltd was incorporated with Joanna Kaunang holding 10% of its shares, reflecting her capital contribution.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Joanna Kaunang’s participation in the formation and funding of Quest Horizon Pte Ltd, involvement in the Niado Technology Pte Ltd venture, and her role and departure from Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, including her summary termination, are consistent with her contractual and implied duties as an employee or give rise to liability or entitlement to damages for dismissal.
Party_Details.Rule: Employees are express contractual terms and an implied duty of fidelity and mutual trust and confidence, which requires them to act honestly towards their employer, not to engage in actual competition during employment, and not to misuse confidential information, while allowing some preparatory steps for future business ventures. dismissal claims turn on whether the employer terminated the contract in accordance with its terms and applicable law, including whether there was contractual or statutory justification for summary termination.
Party_Details.Application: Joanna Kaunang was a customer service manager in Perth, later acting general manager in Singapore, and was positioned to become managing director of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd. While still employed, she initiated the creation of the new-venture-2011 Google Group, proposed names for a new company, became a 10% shareholder in Quest Horizon Pte Ltd upon its incorporation on 21 February 2011, and participated in increasing its capital to fund office premises and renovation. She also agreed to participate in the Niado Technology Pte Ltd software venture, with Quest Horizon Pte Ltd taking a substantial shareholding, and Quest Horizon Pte Ltd entered into a tenancy for office space at Changi Business Park on 31 May 2011. She resigned on 21 September 2011 and was summarily terminated on 21 November 2011 while serving notice. These facts provide the basis for assessing whether her investment and organisational activities amounted to permissible preparatory steps or conflicted with her employment obligations, and whether Griffin Travel Pte Ltd’s summary termination complied with the contractual framework, thereby affecting her counterclaim for dismissal.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment notes that Joanna Kaunang did not have a written employment contract after her posting to Singapore and that her employment was governed by an implied contract. It records her roles in Perth and Singapore, her intended succession to the managing director position, and her resignation and subsequent summary dismissal. The court describes her involvement in the formation of Quest Horizon Pte Ltd, including asking Leny Widjaja to set up the Google Group, proposing the name 'Quest Horizon', and holding a 10% share. It also notes her participation in the Niado Technology Pte Ltd venture and Quest Horizon’s leasing of office space. In analysing the alleged masterplan, the court treats Joanna as one of several employees who pooled funds for investment and assisted in setting up Quest Horizon and Niado, and it evaluates whether these activities show a coordinated scheme to compete with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd or are consistent with a private investment initiative. The court’s reasoning on the implied duty of fidelity and the permissibility of preparatory steps for competition applies to Joanna’s situation, but the excerpt does not set out the final determination on her liability or on her counterclaim for wrongful dismissal.

Party_Details.Name: Leny Widjaja

Facts.Text: Leny Widjaja was an executive director of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
On 2 February 2011, Leny Widjaja resigned from her employment with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
Leny Widjaja's last day with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd was 30 June 2011, and she resigned from the board of directors on the same day.
In January 2011, Leny Widjaja set up an online discussion group named new-venture-2011@googlegroups.com.

In late 2010, Leny Widjaja had discussions with some colleagues about pooling money for investment, including possible investment in property in Thailand, and she declined to participate because she already had plans to invest in her father's vegetable export business in Medan.
In March 2011, Leny asked Sandeep of Lantone to set up an email domain for Quest Horizon.
By this time, Leny had already resigned and was serving the tail end of her notice period, and she had an active role in the renovation of the Changi Offices.
Leny asked Nagender to put her in touch with D'Perception, and Nagender called Andy, who was its owner.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, as an executive director and later departing employee, Leny Widjaja’s involvement in setting up communication channels for Quest Horizon Pte Ltd, assisting with its office arrangements and renovation, and her own investment decisions are consistent with her and contractual obligations to Griffin Travel Pte Ltd or contribute to any liability in relation to the masterplan and competitive activity.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors owe duties to act in good faith in what they consider to be the best interests of the company, to avoid unauthorised conflicts of interest, and not to exploit corporate opportunities for personal benefit without informed consent. Employees, including senior managers, owe an implied duty of fidelity and mutual trust and confidence, which restricts actual competition and misuse of confidential information during employment but allows some preparatory steps for future ventures. The assessment of any depends on the nature of the director’s or employee’s role, the extent of their decision-making authority, and the specific conduct in question.
Party_Details.Application: Leny Widjaja was an executive director of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd until 30 June 2011 and thus owed director-level obligations during that period. She participated in early 2011 discussions about pooling funds for investment but chose not to invest in Quest Horizon Pte Ltd, instead planning to invest in a family business. She created the new-venture-2011 Google Group at Joanna Kaunang’s request, arranged for Quest Horizon email accounts to be set up, and later, while serving notice, assisted Quest Horizon Pte Ltd with renovation planning and engaged D’Perception Singapore Pte Ltd for the Changi Business Park offices. These activities occurred while she was still a director and employee and involved organisational support for a company in which she was not a shareholder. The factual pattern is determining whether she facilitated a competing structure or simply assisted colleagues with a private investment project, and whether any such assistance conflicted with her obligations to Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court recognises that Leny Widjaja, as an executive director of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, owed fiduciary duties while in office. It notes that her 2006 employment agreement referred to the Staff Manual and that the 2009 employment contract changed this reference to a Policy Manual, which is part of the reasoning that the Staff Manual was not incorporated. The judgment records that Leny declined to invest in Quest Horizon Pte Ltd, intending instead to invest in her father’s business, but that she helped set up the Google Group and later played an active role in the renovation of the Changi Business Park offices for Quest Horizon and Niado. The court compares her situation with authorities on fiduciary duties and middle management roles, ultimately concluding that she, as a director, did owe fiduciary duties, but the excerpt does not detail the final findings on whether her specific conduct in relation to Quest Horizon and Niado amounted to a breach or contributed to liability under the alleged masterplan.

Party_Details.Name: Pereira Rahul Anthony

Facts.Text: Pereira Rahul Anthony was the Plaintiff’s manager of operations and resigned on 22 September 2011.
On 22 September 2011, Pereira Rahul Anthony resigned from his employment with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Pereira Rahul Anthony’s conduct as manager of operations and his resignation and subsequent summary termination give rise to liability to Griffin Travel Pte Ltd or entitle him to relief for dismissal.
Party_Details.Rule: An employee’s obligations are governed by express contractual terms and the implied duty of fidelity and mutual trust and confidence, which restrict actual competition and misuse of confidential information during employment. dismissal claims depend on whether the employer complied with contractual and legal requirements for termination, including any grounds for summary dismissal.
Party_Details.Application: The available facts Pereira Rahul Anthony served as manager of operations at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, resigned on 22 September 2011, and was summarily terminated on 6 October 2011 while serving notice. The judgment excerpt does not detail his involvement, if any, in the formation or operation of Quest Horizon Pte Ltd, Niado Technology Pte Ltd, or other new entities. These limited facts frame the assessment of whether his termination was consistent with his contract and the implied duty framework, and whether he has a basis for a dismissal counterclaim.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment identifies Pereira Rahul Anthony as manager of operations who resigned and was later summarily dismissed on allegations of gross misconduct, and notes that he counterclaimed for wrongful dismissal. The excerpt provided does not elaborate on his specific conduct in relation to the alleged masterplan or new entities, nor does it set out the court’s final reasoning on his liability or on the merits of his wrongful dismissal counterclaim.

Party_Details.Name: Gwee Bee Ting Annie

Facts.Text: Gwee Bee Ting Annie was the Plaintiff’s general manager of operations and was in Band 4.
On 12 September 2011, Gwee Bee Ting Annie resigned from her employment with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
On 6 October 2011, Griffin Travel Pte Ltd sent a letter to Gwee Bee Ting Annie terminating her employment on allegations of gross misconduct while she was serving her notice period.
On 21 February 2011, Quest Horizon Pte Ltd was incorporated with Annie as one of its directors and a 10% shareholder.
On 27 April 2011, Niado was incorporated with Annie as one of its directors.
Around mid-April 2011, Annie, together with Adella, began sourcing for business premises at Changi Business Park for Niado and Quest Horizon.
On 31 May 2011, Niado signed a tenancy agreement for unit no 08-04 at Changi Business Park.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Gwee Bee Ting Annie’s roles as general manager of operations at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd and as director and shareholder in Quest Horizon Pte Ltd and Niado Technology Pte Ltd, along with her resignation and summary termination, are consistent with her contractual and implied duties or give rise to liability or a valid dismissal claim.
Party_Details.Rule: Senior employees owe an implied duty of fidelity and mutual trust and confidence, which includes not engaging in actual competition with their employer during employment and not misusing confidential information, while allowing some preparatory steps for future ventures. Their express contractual terms may further regulate outside directorships and secondary employment. dismissal claims depend on whether the employer had contractual or legal grounds for summary termination and followed the agreed procedures.
Party_Details.Application: Gwee Bee Ting Annie held a senior operational role at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd while also becoming a director and 10% shareholder of Quest Horizon Pte Ltd upon its incorporation on 21 February 2011 and a director of Niado Technology Pte Ltd upon its incorporation on 27 April 2011. She assisted in sourcing and securing office premises at Changi Business Park for these entities and later resigned from Griffin Travel Pte Ltd on 12 September 2011 before being summarily terminated on 6 October 2011. These facts are central to assessing whether her external directorships and involvement in new entities constituted permissible preparatory activity or conflicted with any contractual restrictions on secondary employment and her implied duty of fidelity, and whether Griffin Travel Pte Ltd’s summary termination was contractually justified.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment notes that Gwee Bee Ting Annie was in Band 4 and subordinate to Adella, indicating that Band 4 was not reserved solely for top management. It records her roles as director and shareholder of Quest Horizon Pte Ltd and director of Niado Technology Pte Ltd, her involvement in sourcing premises at Changi Business Park, and her resignation and subsequent summary dismissal. In the broader analysis of the alleged masterplan, the court treats her as one of the core participants in Quest Horizon and Niado and evaluates whether their activities show a coordinated competitive scheme or a private investment and software venture. The court’s reasoning on the implied duty of fidelity, the permissibility of preparatory steps, and the non-incorporation of the Staff Manual’s restrictions into the 2009 employment contracts informs the assessment of her conduct, but the excerpt does not provide the final conclusions on her liability or wrongful dismissal counterclaim.

Party_Details.Name: Ng Choo Geok Adella

Facts.Text: As at early 2011, Ng Choo Geok Adella was director of operations at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, classified in Band 4, and oversaw operations staff in multiple locations including Bangkok, Perth and Kuala Lumpur.
On 24 August 2011, Ng Choo Geok Adella resigned from her employment with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
On 6 October 2011, Griffin Travel Pte Ltd sent a letter to Ng Choo Geok Adella terminating her employment on allegations of gross misconduct while she was serving her notice period.
In late 2010, Ng Choo Geok Adella discussed with colleagues including Gwee Bee Ting Annie and Madhu Kumar the idea of pooling money for investment, initially considering property in Thailand.
On 21 February 2011, Quest Horizon Pte Ltd was incorporated with Ng Choo Geok Adella as a director and 35% shareholder, reflecting her capital contribution.
Around mid-April 2011, Ng Choo Geok Adella, together with Gwee Bee Ting Annie, began sourcing for business premises at Changi Business Park for Quest Horizon Pte Ltd and Niado Technology Pte Ltd.
On 31 May 2011, Quest Horizon Pte Ltd, with Ng Choo Geok Adella as a director, signed a tenancy agreement for unit 08-03 at Changi Business Park.

By May 2011, Ng Choo Geok Adella and the other shareholders of Quest Horizon Pte Ltd increased its share capital from SGD 250,000 to SGD 375,000 to fund rental and renovation costs for office premises.

By late March 2011, after being approached by Ciocan Cornel, Ng Choo Geok Adella agreed with colleagues to participate in the software venture that became Niado Technology Pte Ltd, with Quest Horizon Pte Ltd taking a 45% shareholding.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Ng Choo Geok Adella’s conduct as director of operations at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, including her role in initiating and managing Quest Horizon Pte Ltd and Niado Technology Pte Ltd and her resignation and summary termination, is consistent with her contractual and implied duties or gives rise to liability or a valid dismissal claim.
Party_Details.Rule: Senior employees who are not directors generally owe an implied duty of fidelity and mutual trust and confidence, which restricts actual competition and misuse of confidential information during employment but allows some preparatory steps for future ventures. The existence of duties depends on the nature and scope of the employee’s role and decision-making authority. dismissal claims depend on whether the employer had contractual or legal grounds for summary termination and complied with the agreed procedures.
Party_Details.Application: Ng Choo Geok Adella held a senior operational role at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, classified in Band 4, and was involved in supervising operations across several locations. In late 2010 and early 2011, she initiated discussions about pooling funds for investment, became a 35% shareholder and director of Quest Horizon Pte Ltd upon its incorporation on 21 February 2011, and later helped source and secure office premises at Changi Business Park for Quest Horizon and Niado Technology Pte Ltd. She also agreed to participate in the Niado venture through Quest Horizon’s 45% shareholding. She resigned from Griffin Travel Pte Ltd on 24 August 2011 and was summarily terminated on 6 October 2011 while serving notice. These facts are central to determining whether her external directorship and investment activities constituted permissible preparatory steps or conflicted with her obligations to Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, and whether the summary termination was contractually justified.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considers whether Ng Choo Geok Adella owed fiduciary duties and concludes that, despite her senior Band 4 role and title of director of operations, she was part of middle management and did not owe fiduciary obligations akin to a director. It notes that she coordinated communications with suppliers but did not have authority to decide contract terms or to both hire and fire staff. The judgment records her central role in initiating the investment pooling idea, her 35% shareholding and directorship in Quest Horizon Pte Ltd, her involvement in sourcing premises and signing the tenancy for the Changi Business Park office, and her participation in the Niado Technology Pte Ltd venture. In analysing the alleged masterplan, the court evaluates whether these activities show a coordinated scheme to compete with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd or are consistent with a private investment and software project. The reasoning on the implied duty of fidelity and the non-existence of a general duty to report colleagues’ plans informs the assessment of her conduct, but the excerpt does not provide the final conclusions on her liability or wrongful dismissal counterclaim.

Party_Details.Name: Narra Gouri Prasad

Facts.Text: On 1 March 2011, Narra Gouri Prasad resigned from his employment with Griffin Travel Pte Ltd.
As at 2011, Narra Gouri Prasad was the brother-in-law of Nagender Rao Chilkuri.
In early 2011, Narra Gouri Prasad assisted Quest Horizon Pte Ltd by liaising with IP Consultants Pte Ltd, which Quest Horizon engaged as its corporate secretary, in relation to incorporation matters.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Narra Gouri Prasad’s conduct as Vice President – Finance at Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, including his assistance to Quest Horizon Pte Ltd and his resignation, is consistent with his contractual and implied duties or gives rise to liability in relation to the masterplan.
Party_Details.Rule: Senior finance employees owe an implied duty of fidelity and mutual trust and confidence, which includes acting honestly, not engaging in actual competition with their employer during employment, and not misusing confidential information. The existence of duties depends on the scope of their authority, including whether they have general management powers or the ability to make decisions that materially affect the company’s interests.
Party_Details.Application: Narra Gouri Prasad served as Vice President – Finance and effectively chief financial officer of Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, with Band 4 classification and account signatory status, but without authority to approve expenses or decide on budget allocations . He resigned on 1 March 2011 and remained until 31 August 2011. During this period, he assisted Quest Horizon Pte Ltd by liaising with IP Consultants Pte Ltd on incorporation-related matters. These facts are determining whether his assistance to Quest Horizon Pte Ltd constituted permissible support for a private investment venture or conflicted with his obligations to Griffin Travel Pte Ltd, and whether his role and authority were such that duties should be imposed.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examines whether Narra Gouri Prasad owed fiduciary duties and concludes that, despite his Band 4 classification and effective CFO role, he did not have powers to approve expenses or decide on budget allocations and was part of middle management. Relying on authorities such as Nagase and Shepherd Andrew, the judge finds that he did not owe fiduciary duties to Griffin Travel Pte Ltd. The judgment notes his assistance to Quest Horizon Pte Ltd in liaising with IP Consultants Pte Ltd and his familial relationship with Nagender Rao Chilkuri, but rejects the plaintiff’s unpleaded suggestion that Madhu Kumar was a proxy for Nagender. The excerpt does not set out the final conclusions on Prasad’s liability in relation to the alleged masterplan.

Party_Details.Name: Signature Sparks Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Signature Sparks Pte Ltd is the investment holding company through which Nagender Rao Chilkuri subscribed to shares in Griffin Global Holdco Limited and a loan note in Griffin Bidco Limited under a subscription agreement dated 16 December 2009.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Signature Sparks Pte Ltd’s shareholding and loan note interests in Griffin Global Holdco Limited and Griffin Bidco Limited, and its use of shared office space, are affected by Griffin Travel Pte Ltd’s allegations against Nagender Rao Chilkuri and the classification of his departure under leaver provisions.
Party_Details.Rule: Shareholder and loan note rights under group restructuring and investment agreements are governed by the terms of those agreements, including any leaver provisions that may adjust entitlements based on the circumstances of the individual’s departure, such as classification as a Good Leaver or Bad Leaver.
Party_Details.Application: Signature Sparks Pte Ltd holds a 9.1% shareholding in Griffin Global Holdco Limited and a GBP 1.6 million loan note in Griffin Bidco Limited under the 16 December 2009 subscription agreement. It later shared office space at Changi Business Park with Quest Horizon Pte Ltd and other entities. Nagender Rao Chilkuri and Signature Sparks Pte Ltd counterclaimed for a declaration that he was a Good Leaver and for payment of the fair value of the Holdco shares and the nominal value plus accrued interest on the Bidco loan stock. These facts frame the assessment of how the leaver provisions in the subscription and related agreements apply to Signature Sparks Pte Ltd’s interests in light of the findings on Nagender’s conduct and departure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment records that Signature Sparks Pte Ltd is the vehicle through which Nagender Rao Chilkuri holds his share and loan note interests in Griffin Global Holdco Limited and Griffin Bidco Limited. It notes that Nagender and Signature Sparks Pte Ltd counterclaimed against Holdco and Bidco for a declaration that he was a Good Leaver and for valuation and payment of his equity and loan note. The court’s analysis of Nagender’s conduct, the alleged masterplan, and the classification of his departure under the group’s leaver provisions will determine Signature Sparks Pte Ltd’s entitlements, but the excerpt does not provide the final conclusions on these issues.
